prompt: A 17th-century allegorical scientific copperplate engraving in the style of early modern encyclopedic frontispieces and natural philosophy diagrams. Depict a grand symmetrical architectural composition with classical columns, plinths, and ornate borders framing a central mountainous landscape. The landscape is rendered as a continuous terrain with slopes, ridges, and a clearly defined basin at the lowest point, representing a natural surface. The terrain must be engraved using precise cross-hatching and stippling, with clean, parallel and intersecting lines that follow the contours of the land, accurately simulating traditional burin engraving techniques. Small human figures (scholars and surveyors) are shown physically descending along winding paths carved into the terrain, moving step-by-step toward the central basin. Their movement should clearly indicate a gradual downward progression. Include period-accurate allegorical elements: robed figures observing or guiding the descent, a compass rose, and a scale bar integrated into the composition. All cartouches and inscription areas must contain only ornamental or illegible script-like markings, not readable text. No trees, no branching structures, no discrete hierarchy. The scene must communicate a continuous surface. No modern text, no English, no mathematical symbols, no axes, no numbers, no graphs. Rendered in monochrome ink on aged parchment, using only precise cross-hatching and stippling. No gradients, no soft shading, no painterly effects, no digital artifacts. The composition should feel like a formal scientific plate from the 17th century, with balanced symmetry, clear hierarchy, and authentic engraving grammar.
tradition_approach: Architectural frontispiece composition with allegorical figures and a terrain-based metaphor using engraved cross-hatching to represent continuous optimization without modern graph structures
